source: CIS AMAZON WEB SERVICES FOUNDATIONS BENCHMARK V5.0.0
title: Ensure multi-factor authentication (MFA) is enabled for all IAM users that have a console password

input_row.source: CIS AMAZON WEB SERVICES FOUNDATIONS BENCHMARK V5.0.0
input_row.title: Ensure multi-factor authentication (MFA) is enabled for all IAM users that have a console password
input_row.description: Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) adds an extra layer of authentication assurance beyond traditional credentials. With MFA enabled, when a user signs in to the AWS Console, they will be prompted for their user name and password as well as for an authentication code from their physical or virtual MFA token. It is recommended that MFA be enabled for all accounts that have a console password.
input_row.rationale: Enabling MFA provides increased security for console access as it requires the authenticating principal to possess a device that displays a time-sensitive key and have knowledge of a credential. Impact: AWS will soon end support for SMS multi-factor authentication (MFA). New customers are not allowed to use this feature. We recommend that existing customers switch to an alternative method of MFA.
input_row.audit: Perform the following to determine if a MFA device is enabled for all IAM users having a console password: From Console: 1. Open the IAM console at https://console.aws.amazon.com/iam/. 2. In the left pane, select Users 3. If the MFA or Password age columns are not visible in the table, click the gear icon at the upper right corner of the table and ensure a checkmark is next to both, then click Close. 4. Ensure that for each user where the Password age column shows a password age, the MFA column shows Virtual, U2F Security Key, or Hardware. From Command Line: 1. Run the following command (OSX/Linux/UNIX) to generate a list of all IAM users along with their password and MFA status: aws iam generate-credential-report aws iam get-credential-report --query 'Content' --output text | base64 -d | cut -d, -f1,4,8 2. The output of this command will produce a table similar to the following: user,password_enabled,mfa_active elise,false,false brandon,true,true rakesh,false,false helene,false,false paras,true,true anitha,false,false 3. For any column having password_enabled set to true , ensure mfa_active is also set to true.
input_row.remediation: Perform the following to enable MFA: From Console: 1. Sign in to the AWS Management Console and open the IAM console at 'https://console.aws.amazon.com/iam/' 2. In the left pane, select Users. 3. In the User Name list, choose the name of the intended MFA user. 4. Choose the Security Credentials tab, and then choose Manage MFA Device. 5. In the Manage MFA Device wizard, choose Virtual MFA device, and then choose Continue. IAM generates and displays configuration information for the virtual MFA device, including a QR code graphic. The graphic is a representation of the 'secret configuration key' that is available for manual entry on devices that do not support QR codes.  6. Open your virtual MFA application. (For a list of apps that you can use for hosting virtual MFA devices, see Virtual MFA Applications at https://aws.amazon.com/iam/details/mfa/#Virtual_MFA_Applications). If the virtual MFA application supports multiple accounts (multiple virtual MFA devices), choose the option to create a new account (a new virtual MFA device). 7. Determine whether the MFA app supports QR codes, and then do one of the following: • Use the app to scan the QR code. For example, you might choose the camera icon or choose an option similar to Scan code, and then use the device's camera to scan the code. • In the Manage MFA Device wizard, choose Show secret key for manual configuration, and then type the secret configuration key into your MFA application. When you are finished, the virtual MFA device starts generating one-time passwords. 8. In the Manage MFA Device wizard, in the MFA Code 1 box, type the one-time password that currently appears in the virtual MFA device. Wait up to 30 seconds for the device to generate a new one-time password. Then type the second one-time password into the MFA Code 2 box. 9. Click Assign MFA. References: 1. https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6238 2. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_credentials_mfa.html 3. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/best-practices.html#enable- mfa-for-privileged-users 4. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_credentials_mfa_enable_ virtual.html 5. CCE-78901-6 6. https://blogs.aws.amazon.com/security/post/Tx2SJJYE082KBUK/How-to- Delegate-Management-of-Multi-Factor-Authentication-to-AWS-IAM-Users Additional Information: Forced IAM User Self-Service Remediation Amazon has published a pattern that requires users to set up MFA through self-service before they gain access to their complete set of permissions. Until they complete this step, they cannot access their full permissions. This pattern can be used for new AWS accounts. It can also be applied to existing accounts; it is recommended that users receive instructions and a grace period to complete MFA enrollment before active enforcement on existing AWS accounts.
input_row.impact: AWS will soon end support for SMS multi-factor authentication (MFA). New customers are not allowed to use this feature. We recommend that existing customers switch to an alternative method of MFA.
input_row.references: 1. https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6238 2. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_credentials_mfa.html 3. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/best-practices.html#enable- mfa-for-privileged-users 4. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_credentials_mfa_enable_ virtual.html 5. CCE-78901-6 6. https://blogs.aws.amazon.com/security/post/Tx2SJJYE082KBUK/How-to- Delegate-Management-of-Multi-Factor-Authentication-to-AWS-IAM-Users Additional Information: Forced IAM User Self-Service Remediation Amazon has published a pattern that requires users to set up MFA through self-service before they gain access to their complete set of permissions. Until they complete this step, they cannot access their full permissions. This pattern can be used for new AWS accounts. It can also be applied to existing accounts; it is recommended that users receive instructions and a grace period to complete MFA enrollment before active enforcement on existing AWS accounts.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**

- **Automated:** The control can be fully verified using AWS APIs and CLI commands. The audit instructions specify using the AWS CLI to generate and retrieve a credential report, which can be queried to check both the password and MFA status of IAM users.

**PROGRAM NAME:** aws_iam_user_mfa_enabled_if_console_password_set

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**

- **AWS API calls or boto3 SDK methods to use:**
  - Use `boto3` to interact with the IAM service.
  - Call `generate_credential_report()` to initiate the creation of a credential report.
  - Use `get_credential_report()` to retrieve the report, which provides details on IAM users, including their password and MFA status.

- **AWS CLI commands (if applicable):**
  - `aws iam generate-credential-report` to generate the report.
  - `aws iam get-credential-report --query 'Content' --output text | base64 -d` to retrieve and decode the credential report.

- **Specific resources to enumerate:**
  - Enumerate all IAM users within the AWS account to verify their MFA and password status.

- **Exact configuration properties/settings to validate:**
  - Check that for each IAM user where `password_enabled` is set to `true`, `mfa_active` is also set to `true` in the credential report.

- **Clear Pass/Fail criteria with expected values:**
  - **Pass:** For any user with `password_enabled` set to `true`, `mfa_active` must also be set to `true`.
  - **Fail:** If any user has `password_enabled` set to `true` and `mfa_active` set to `false`. 

This automated approach ensures that MFA is enabled for all IAM users that have a console password, reinforcing the security posture of the AWS account according to CIS recommendations.
step2_review:
**APPROACH VALIDATION:** AGREE

- The classification of the audit approach as Automated is correct. The control can be effectively verified using AWS APIs and CLI commands. The use of the credential report, which provides comprehensive details about IAM users, including their password and MFA status, is appropriate for this control.

**TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 9

- The automation details are technically accurate. The use of `boto3` methods `generate_credential_report()` and `get_credential_report()` is correct for generating and retrieving the credential report. The AWS CLI commands provided are valid and correctly decode the credential report for analysis.
- The program name `aws_iam_user_mfa_enabled_if_console_password_set` is descriptive and follows a logical naming convention, though it could be slightly more concise.

**IMPROVEMENTS:**

- The program name could be simplified while maintaining clarity, such as `aws_iam_user_mfa_check_with_console_password`.
- Consider adding error handling in the script for scenarios where the credential report generation might fail or take longer than expected.
- It might be beneficial to include a check for the age of the credential report to ensure it is up-to-date before proceeding with the analysis.
- As an alternative, AWS Config could be used to continuously monitor this control, although it would require additional setup and might not be as straightforward as the credential report approach.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

- The assessment is well-founded, and the use of AWS APIs and CLI commands is appropriate for the control being audited. The technical details are accurate, and the approach is consistent with AWS security best practices.

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION:**

- Keep as-is, with a minor suggestion to consider a more concise program name and to ensure error handling and report freshness checks are implemented in the automation script. The current approach effectively meets the control requirements and aligns with AWS best practices for security compliance.

step3_final_decision.decision_text:
**FINAL APPROACH:** Automated

**JUSTIFICATION:**
Both AI opinions correctly identify that this control can be fully audited using AWS APIs. The AWS IAM credential report exposes both the presence of a console password and the MFA status for each IAM user, allowing for programmatic verification. No manual inspection is required, as all necessary data is available via boto3 or AWS CLI.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

---

**PROGRAM NAME:** 
aws_iam_user_mfa_enabled_with_console_password

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- AWS Resource/Service: IAM Users
- API/CLI Method: 
  - boto3: iam.generate_credential_report(), iam.get_credential_report()
  - AWS CLI: aws iam generate-credential-report, aws iam get-credential-report
- Properties to Check: 
  - For each user in the credential report, check:
    - `password_enabled` (should be "true")
    - `mfa_active` (should be "true" if password_enabled is "true")
- Pass Criteria: 
  - Every IAM user with `password_enabled` set to "true" also has `mfa_active` set to "true".
- Fail Criteria: 
  - Any IAM user with `password_enabled` set to "true" and `mfa_active` set to "false".

---

**DISAGREEMENTS RESOLVED:**
There were no substantive disagreements between the AI opinions; both supported automation. The final program name is slightly refined for clarity and specificity.

**SOURCE:** CIS AMAZON WEB SERVICES FOUNDATIONS BENCHMARK V5.0.0
